criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the SFT and RLHF background, including the SFT loss (Eq. 1), the reward modeling loss, and the RLHF PPO objective with KL regularization (Eq. 2).

The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the task formulation: a derived prompt x' is a transformation of the original prompt x that maintains close relevance and improved expression without necessarily being a rewritten prompt.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the self-instructed RL objective, including the design of the DPG instruction x_DPG and the training objective in Eq. 5.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 to understand the full training loop: initialization of pi_theta and pi_ref, sampling from D, obtaining X = Concat([x_DPG, x]), generating x', querying M for y', computing reward and KL penalty, and updating pi_theta.

The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the ICL query framework: using (x, x', y') to fill an in-context query template that instructs the LLM to emulate the quality and style of y' when responding to x.

The agent has read Algorithm 2 to understand the full inference pipeline: generating x' from trained pi, querying LLM with x' to get y', filling the ICL template with (x, x', y'), and querying LLM for the final response.

The agent has read Section 5.1 to check the experimental setup: training dataset (BPO 14K), evaluation datasets (Dolly, Vicuna, Self-Instruct, BPO-test), derived prompt generation models (Llama3, Llama2, Qwen2), reward model, query models, baselines, and evaluation metrics.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the comprehensive main experiment results comparing OURS against OP, BPO, PAS, and SR across four evaluation datasets and multiple model configurations.

The agent has read Appendix B.3 to check all training hyperparameters: ReMax optimizer, DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, temperature tau=0.0, top_p=0.9, max_length=1024, 4 A100 GPUs, LR=1e-6, 2 epochs with decay, KL penalty beta=0.05, batch_size=1.

The agent has read Appendix B.1 to check the dataset descriptions: Dolly Eval (200 samples, 8 categories), Vicuna Eval (80 questions, 8 categories), Self-Instruct Eval (252 user-centered instructions), BPO-test Eval (200 samples).

The plan includes implementing the DPG instruction template x_DPG, which manually instructs the model to generate a more comprehensive, easily understandable, and answerable version of the original question without limiting the response scope.

The plan includes implementing the self-instructed RL training objective (Eq. 5) that maximizes R(x', y') minus the KL penalty beta * log(pi_theta(x'|x) / pi_ref(x'|x)) without requiring any pre-collected (x, x') pairs.

The plan includes implementing Algorithm 1: the complete training loop with initialization of both pi_theta and pi_ref from the same base LLM, sampling original prompts from D, generating derived prompts, querying the frozen response model, and performing gradient updates.

The plan includes implementing Algorithm 2 for inference: generating x' via the trained DPG model, querying LLM with x' to obtain y', and constructing an ICL demonstration using the (x, x', y') triple.

The plan includes implementing the in-context query template that presents (x', y') as an example and instructs the LLM to emulate the same response quality, style, and detail when answering the original prompt x.
The plan includes loading the BPO training dataset of 14K diverse samples as the training data D for optimizing pi_theta.
The plan includes loading all four evaluation datasets: Dolly Eval (200 samples), Vicuna Eval (80 questions), Self-Instruct Eval (252 instructions), and BPO-test Eval (200 samples).
The plan includes training pi_theta on three base models: Llama3-8B-Instruct (Llama3), Llama2-7B-chat (Llama2), and Qwen2-7B-Instruct (Qwen2).
The plan includes using both black-box query models (GPT4, GPT3.5) and white-box query models (Llama3-8B, Llama2-7B, Qwen2-7B) as the frozen response model M during training and inference.
The plan includes implementing a reward model trained on the hh-rlhf helpful dataset to evaluate the quality of (x', y') pairs during RL training.
The plan includes implementing all three baseline methods: BPO (SFT-based prompt refinement), PAS (plug-and-play prompt augmentation), and Self-Refine (iterative self-feedback).
The plan includes the ablation experiments: OD alone (without ICL), ICL applied to BPO and PAS, and the w/o training vs SFT vs RL comparison.

The plan includes using GPT4 as an LLM-judge with MT-bench evaluation prompts, implementing random shuffling for each evaluation round to mitigate position bias, and computing Win Rate (WR) as (A Win - B Win)%.

The plan includes all training hyperparameters from Appendix B.3: ReMax optimizer, LR=1e-6, 2 epochs with decay, beta=0.05, batch_size=1, tau=0.0, top_p=0.9, max_length=1024, 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with DeepSpeed ZeRO-2.

The DPG instruction string x_DPG has been implemented exactly as specified: instructing the model to provide a more comprehensive, easily understandable, and answerable version of the question, adding contextual information, avoiding limiting understanding to a few aspects, allowing multi-angle thinking, and returning only the refined question.
The concatenation operation X = Concat([x_DPG, x]) has been implemented as Eq. 4, prepending the DPG instruction to the original prompt before feeding it to pi_theta.

The self-instructed RL training objective (Eq. 3 / Eq. 5) has been implemented: maximizing the expected reward R(x', y') minus the KL divergence penalty beta * log(pi_theta(x'|x) / pi_ref(x'|x)) over sampled x ~ D, x' ~ pi_theta(x'|x), y' ~ M(y'|x').
Algorithm 1 has been implemented: initialization of both pi_theta and pi_ref identically from the same base LLM pi before training begins.
Algorithm 1 has been implemented: random sampling of original prompt x from training dataset D, constructing X = Concat([x_DPG, x]), and sampling the derived prompt x' ~ pi_theta(x'|X).
Algorithm 1 has been implemented: querying the frozen response model M with the derived prompt x' to generate response y' ~ M(y'|x').
The KL penalty term beta * log(pi_theta(x'|X) / pi_ref(x'|X)) has been correctly implemented as a regularization term to prevent pi_theta from diverging too far from pi_ref during RL training.
The reward computation R(x', y') has been implemented using the reward model to evaluate the quality of the derived prompt x' and response y' pair.
The gradient update step of Algorithm 1 has been implemented: maximizing [R(x', y') - beta * log(pi_theta(x'|X) / pi_ref(x'|X))] with respect to pi_theta parameters.
The response model M has been properly frozen (no gradient updates) during the training of pi_theta, as specified in Section 4.2.
Algorithm 2 has been implemented for inference: generating derived prompt x' ~ pi(x'|X) using the trained DPG model and the DPG instruction.
Algorithm 2 has been implemented for inference: querying the LLM with derived prompt x' to obtain y' ~ LLM(y'|x'), then filling the in-context query template with (x, x', y').

The in-context query template has been implemented exactly as specified: presenting (x', y') as a demonstration example and instructing the LLM to emulate the response quality, style, and detail level when answering the original prompt x.
The BPO training dataset of 14K diverse samples has been loaded as the training set D for pi_theta, covering single-turn response generation tasks.
All four evaluation datasets have been loaded: Dolly Eval (200 samples), Vicuna Eval (80 questions), Self-Instruct Eval (252 instructions), BPO-test Eval (200 samples).
The Llama3-8B-Instruct model has been implemented and loaded as the primary derived prompt generation model pi_theta (abbreviated as Llama3).
Llama2-7B-chat (Llama2) and Qwen2-7B-Instruct (Qwen2) have been implemented and loaded as additional pi_theta variants for training and evaluation.
The reward model trained on the hh-rlhf helpful dataset has been implemented and integrated into the RL training loop to compute R(x', y').
GPT4 (GPT-4o) and GPT3.5-turbo have been integrated as frozen black-box query models that respond to derived prompts x' during both training and inference.
White-box query models (Llama2-7B-chat, Llama3-8B-Instruct, Qwen2-7B-Instruct) have been implemented as frozen response models M for training and cross-model transferability experiments.
The BPO baseline has been implemented: a prompt refinement model trained via SFT on (original, refined) pairs collected from ChatGPT, which directly replaces the original prompt with the refined one.
The PAS (Plug-and-Play Prompt Augmentation System) baseline has been implemented as a competing prompt refinement method.
The Self-Refine baseline has been implemented: an iterative refinement method that uses the LLM's own feedback to improve response quality.
The ReMax algorithm has been implemented as the RL optimizer for training pi_theta, as specified in Appendix B.3.
Training hyperparameters have been implemented as specified in Appendix B.3: learning rate 1e-6, 2 training epochs with learning rate decay, KL penalty beta=0.05, and batch size 1.

Generation hyperparameters have been implemented as specified in Appendix B.3: temperature tau=0.0, nucleus sampling top_p=0.9, and maximum generation length of 1024 tokens for both derived prompt generation and response model.
DeepSpeed ZeRO-2 has been configured for distributed training on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, as specified in Appendix B.3.

The GPT4-as-judge evaluation pipeline has been implemented using the MT-bench pairwise comparison prompt from Appendix C, where GPT4 evaluates two responses and outputs [[A]], [[B]], or [[C]] for tie.
The Win Rate (WR) metric has been implemented as (A Win - B Win)%, and random shuffling of response order has been implemented for each evaluation round to mitigate position bias.
The OD (Our Derived prompt without ICL) condition has been implemented for ablation: generating x' and using x' directly as input to the LLM without constructing an ICL demonstration.
BPO+ICL and PAS+ICL ablation conditions have been implemented: applying the ICL query framework using BPO/PAS refined prompts as x' instead of OD-generated derived prompts.
The w/o training baseline has been implemented: directly using the pre-trained LLM as the DPG model without any RL or SFT fine-tuning, generating derived prompts via the DPG instruction.
The SFT-based training variant has been implemented for comparison in Figure 2: fine-tuning pi_theta via supervised learning instead of RL to compare against the RL objective.
The reward score evaluation has been implemented using the reward model to assign quantitative scores to responses generated by OURS, OP, and BPO when querying GPT4 (Table 8).
The experiments to run two repeated runs for all main experiments, as stated in Section 5, have been implemented for reliability assessment.

The main experiments in Table 2 have been executed successfully for all combinations of pi_theta (Llama3, Llama2, Qwen2) x query models (GPT4, GPT3.5, Llama3-8B, Llama2-7B, Qwen2-7B) x 4 evaluation datasets, comparing OURS against OP, BPO, PAS, and SR.
The RL training for all three pi_theta models (Llama3, Llama2, Qwen2) has been executed successfully on 4 A100 GPUs with DeepSpeed ZeRO-2 using the BPO training dataset.

The ablation experiments have been executed successfully: OD vs OP/BPO/PAS (Table 3), BPO+ICL and PAS+ICL comparisons vs OP and their base methods (Table 4), and OURS vs BPO+ICL and PAS+ICL (Table 5) on Vicuna Eval and Self-Instruct using Llama3 with GPT4 as query.
The w/o training vs SFT vs RL comparison in Figure 2 has been executed successfully on Vicuna Eval, reporting WR for both OD vs OP and OD+ICL vs OP conditions.
The efficiency analysis has been executed successfully: measuring data-collection requirement, average generation length, and inference time in seconds for OP, BPO, PAS, and OURS (Table 6).
The reward score evaluation (Table 8) has been executed successfully: querying GPT4 with Llama3-based OURS, OP, and BPO, then scoring responses with the reward model on all four evaluation datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: OURS consistently outperforms OP across all four evaluation datasets (Vicuna, BPO-test, Dolly, Self-Instruct) for all pi_theta models and query model combinations.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: OURS consistently outperforms BPO across all four evaluation datasets for all pi_theta and query model combinations.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: when using Llama3 as pi_theta and GPT4 as query, OURS achieves average WRs of approximately 67.1% vs OP and 56.1% vs BPO across the four datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: when using Llama3 as pi_theta and GPT3.5 as query, OURS achieves average WRs of approximately 74.3% vs OP and 69.9% vs BPO across the four datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: OURS demonstrates cross-model transferability, where pi_theta trained on Llama3 improves query quality for Llama2-7B, Llama3-8B, and Qwen2-7B query models.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: the OD method (without ICL) achieves approximately 51.2% WR vs BPO and 33.4% vs PAS on Vicuna Eval, and 33.4% WR vs BPO on Self-Instruct, demonstrating RL objective effectiveness without ICL.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: BPO+ICL achieves higher WR vs OP compared to BPO alone (approximately 7.15% average gain), and BPO+ICL achieves ~59.0% WR vs BPO, demonstrating ICL as a general plug-and-play enhancement.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: PAS+ICL achieves higher WR vs OP compared to PAS alone across both Vicuna Eval and Self-Instruct evaluation datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 5: OURS (OD+ICL) outperforms BPO+ICL with average WRs of approximately 63.9% on Vicuna and 47.5% on Self-Instruct, and outperforms PAS+ICL with approximately 33.9% on Vicuna and 29.2% on Self-Instruct.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 2: RL achieves 67.6% WR (OD vs OP) compared to SFT's 53.8% and no training's 17.6%, and RL achieves 86.2% WR (OD+ICL vs OP) compared to SFT's 65.0% and no training's 39.1% on Vicuna Eval.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 6: OURS is data-collection-free (unlike BPO and PAS), has the longest average generation length (521.6 tokens), and the highest inference time (16.18 seconds).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 8: OURS achieves higher reward scores than OP and BPO on Vicuna Eval (3.97 vs 3.72 and 3.83), BPO-test Eval (3.19 vs 1.89 and 2.42), and Self-Instruct Eval (2.79 vs 0.72 and 1.78).

The execution outcomes match the human evaluation results in Table 9: OURS achieves 86.3% in maintaining original intent, 16.4% in predefined response angle, and 17.9% in introduced bias, outperforming BPO and PAS on all three Q1 metrics.

The execution outcomes match the human evaluation results in Table 10: OURS achieves the highest average scores across coherence (3.8), relevance (3.9), and satisfaction (4.0) compared to OP, Self-Refine, BPO, and PAS.
